name: NYC Office of Technology & Innovation (OTI)
tagline: New York City's consolidated technology agency: OTI runs citywide IT, cybersecurity (NYC Cyber Command), digital services (MyCity, 311, NYC.gov), and broadband — and sets the technical direction and contracts most of the City's ~100 agencies buy through.
overview: The NYC Office of Technology & Innovation (OTI) is the City of New York's consolidated central technology agency, created in 2022 when the legacy Department of Information Technology and Telecommunications (DoITT, the prior account name "NYC Information Technology and Telecommunications") was reorganized and merged with the Mayor's Office of the CTO, NYC Cyber Command, the Mayor's Office of Data Analytics, the Office of Information Privacy, and the Algorithms Management and Policy Officer. OTI sets technology strategy and policy for roughly 100 City agencies and offices, runs shared IT infrastructure (data centers, networks, the Citywide Service Desk), and owns the public-facing digital layer: NYC.gov, the 311 non-emergency system, NYC Open Data, the MyCity benefits/services portal, and the Big Apple Connect broadband program. Cybersecurity for the City sits inside OTI via NYC Cyber Command, which protects and defends systems across all agencies. OTI is best understood as both a large IT operator and the technical/contractual gatekeeper for citywide technology buys — though agency procurement runs through NYC's contracting machinery (ACCOs, MOCS, DCAS) rather than a single state-style cooperative vehicle. A mayoral transition in January 2026 brought a new administration and a new technology leadership team.

budget.intro: OTI is a substantial direct IT spender and operator, not just a policy shop. For FY2026 the agency's expense budget is roughly $743M, supporting ~1,500 positions, plus about $203M in capital for IT modernization, cybersecurity, 311, and emergency-communications upgrades. Citywide capital technology investment tied to OTI runs into the high hundreds of millions to ~$1B across multi-year plans.

stats.label: Capital (FY26)
stats.note: IT modernization, cyber, 311, emergency comms upgrades

stats.label: Big Apple Connect residents
stats.note: Free internet/cable across 220 NYCHA developments; extended to June 2028

notes: NYC's overall FY2026 budget is among the largest municipal budgets in the U.S.; OTI's appropriation is the IT slice, while large agencies (HRA/DSS, NYPD, Health) carry their own application budgets.
Big Apple Connect received a three-year extension in September 2025 (through June 2028), with ~$1.2M in additional provider-funded digital-literacy programming.

FY2026 figures reflect the Adams-era adopted budget. Under Mamdani's first budget cycle, OTI's FY2027 Preliminary Plan was ~$702.6M; the City Council adopted a $125.8B citywide FY2027 budget on June 30, 2026, with savings cited from tech modernization and reduced software-license costs — expect OTI's roadmap to be re-baselined.

initiatives.name: MyCity portal & AI chatbot
initiatives.summary: OTI's flagship single front-door for City services and benefits, including the first citywide AI-powered chatbot. The chatbot expanded in 2025 to pull 311 and broader government-service content; a Jobs NYC portal was added. Modernization, content, and responsible-AI governance are active workstreams.

initiatives.name: NYC AI Action Plan & generative-AI governance
initiatives.summary: OTI published the nation's first comprehensive municipal AI Action Plan (37 actions) and stood up an AI Steering Committee; by FY2025 it had initiated or completed 32 of 37 actions. Generative-AI and computer-vision guidance were advanced in 2025 with updates planned for early 2026 — a governance gate for any AI offering sold into the City.

initiatives.name: Cybersecurity via NYC Cyber Command
initiatives.summary: Cyber Command sits inside OTI and defends ~100+ agencies (identify/protect/detect/respond/recover), runs a vulnerability-disclosure program, and leads citywide incident response. Security infrastructure, identity, and resilience are ongoing investment areas under the City CISO.

initiatives.name: Broadband & digital equity
initiatives.summary: Big Apple Connect (the nation's largest municipally subsidized broadband program, 330K+ NYCHA residents) was extended to June 2028, and OTI released its first Digital Equity Roadmap (11 initiatives) in March 2025. Council scrutiny has pushed OTI toward a broader broadband strategy beyond BAC.

procurement.intro: Unlike a state co-op model, NYC technology buying runs through the City's centralized contracting framework. OTI sets citywide IT direction and leverages the City's buying power through requirement/citywide contracts, while procurement mechanics flow through Agency Chief Contracting Officers (ACCOs), the Mayor's Office of Contract Services (MOCS), and DCAS. Confirm contract vehicles and OTI's cloud-review gate before forecasting.

vehicles.name: OTI citywide / requirement IT contracts
vehicles.detail: OTI leverages the City's buying power so agencies can acquire IT goods and services through pre-established requirement contracts rather than each running its own solicitation — a primary path for hardware, software, and services.

vehicles.name: MOCS & Agency Chief Contracting Officers (ACCOs)
vehicles.detail: The Mayor's Office of Contract Services oversees City procurement; OTI's Citywide ACCO manages competitive bids, RFPs, systems-integrator contracts, and M/WBE participation citywide and on OTI's behalf.

vehicles.name: DCAS (Dept. of Citywide Administrative Services)
vehicles.detail: Runs citywide goods procurement and requirements contracts many agencies buy commodity IT and equipment through — a complementary route alongside OTI-led IT contracts.

vehicles.name: OTI cloud-review gate
vehicles.detail: OTI reviews and must approve cloud-based services before agencies procure/deploy them — clear this technical/security review early for any cloud or as-a-service play touching City data.

powerStructure.name: Lisa Gelobter
powerStructure.title: Chief Technology Officer & OTI Commissioner
powerStructure.note: Appointed by Mayor Mamdani, announced Feb 11, 2026. Former Obama-era Chief Digital Service Officer at the U.S. Dept. of Education; founder of tEQuitable. Top relationship for the citywide tech motion; emphasizes technology for public good, service delivery, and digital equity.

powerStructure.name: Kelly Moan
powerStructure.title: Chief Information Security Officer, City of New York (NYC Cyber Command)
powerStructure.note: Leads OTI's Cyber Command across 100+ agencies. Prior NYPD CISO and a former DHS division chief. The security buying-center owner — verify continuity under the new administration.

powerStructure.name: Mayor Zohran Mamdani
powerStructure.title: Mayor of New York City (took office Jan 2026)
powerStructure.note: New administration setting tech and budget priorities; expect first executive/adopted budgets to reshape OTI's roadmap.

powerStructure.name: OTI Procurement & Vendor Management / Citywide ACCO
powerStructure.title: Citywide IT contracting
powerStructure.note: Owns competitive bids, RFPs, systems-integrator contracts, vendor onboarding, and M/WBE compliance — the path onto OTI/citywide vehicles.

powerStructure.name: Mayor's Office of Contract Services (MOCS)
powerStructure.title: Citywide procurement oversight
powerStructure.note: Sets/oversees City procurement policy and the contract pipeline that OTI and agency buys flow through.

news.title: City Council adopts $125.8B FY2027 budget — Mamdani's first (June 30, 2026)
news.impact: Mamdani's first adopted budget (voted 45-6) sets the new administration's spending baseline; OTI's Preliminary Plan pegged FY2027 at ~$702.6M (down from ~$743M FY26), and citywide plans cite savings from modernizing tech and cutting software-license costs. Re-baseline OTI's roadmap against the new administration's priorities.

news.title: Scrutiny of OTI's multibillion-dollar citywide IT master contracts
news.impact: Reporting flagged OTI overspending ~$3B on two citywide IT master agreements (2018-2025), with CDW Government paid $600M+ this fiscal year (incl. a $142M Chromebook buy) under opaque delivery orders. Mamdani said in March 2026 OTI would renegotiate tech contracts; the City is negotiating two new citywide IT contracts worth ~$2B total — a major access-path event for any hardware/services seller.

news.title: OTI launches 'Get Online NYC' digital-inclusion campaign
news.impact: New nyc.gov/getonline site, interactive map, and multilingual navigators point residents to free Wi-Fi, device lending, and low-cost internet across 450+ sites — extends the Digital Equity Roadmap and sustains broadband/device demand; earned NDIA's Visionary Trailblazer award.

news.title: Comptroller audit faults OTI's MyCity system
news.impact: Comptroller audit (released Dec 30, 2025) calls MyCity a poor ROI after $100M+ spent — limited integrated functionality, weak vendor oversight, poor portal performance, and an AI chatbot with 71% negative feedback; recommends a feasibility review. Signals openness to re-architecting citywide digital services and AI-ready platforms.

news.title: Mayor Mamdani names Lisa Gelobter NYC CTO & OTI Commissioner
news.impact: New tech leadership at the top of the citywide motion (announced Feb 11, 2026); priorities still forming — re-engage and re-map the relationship chart early.

news.title: Mamdani administration takes office; Matt Fraser departs as CTO
news.impact: Mayoral transition resets tech priorities and budget direction; CTO seat was vacant before Gelobter's appointment.

dellActions: Re-engage early under the new Mamdani administration and Lisa Gelobter's OTI — leadership priorities and the first budget are still forming, so build the relationship map before the roadmap hardens.
Confirm Dell's position on OTI citywide/requirement IT contracts and DCAS commodity vehicles — the access paths to ~100 agencies, not just OTI itself.
Lead with infrastructure and AI-ready compute (ISG) tied to MyCity, the AI Action Plan, and 311/digital-services modernization — and clear OTI's cloud-review and responsible-AI governance gates.
Build the security relationship with NYC Cyber Command and City CISO Kelly Moan; align to resilience, identity, and incident-response investment.
Tie hardware and connectivity offers to broadband/digital-equity demand (Big Apple Connect, Digital Equity Roadmap) extended through 2028.
